description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).
teaching_value: Gives students a mental map of the entire agent system before diving into individual components, making it easier to contextualize each subsystem (planning, memory, tools) as part of a coherent whole.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall system structure; return to it as a reference when introducing each individual component.
suggested_caption: LLM-powered autonomous agent system overview. (Weng, 2023)

description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how interleaving reasoning traces with actions differs from action-only approaches, helping students understand why the Thought step improves performance on both knowledge retrieval and sequential decision tasks.
when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the ReAct framework; use to contrast Act-only vs. ReAct trajectories and prompt discussion on why explicit reasoning steps matter.
suggested_caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)

description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle of: Actor generating actions, Evaluator scoring trajectories, Self-Reflection module producing verbal feedback, and that feedback being stored in episodic memory to inform the next trial.
teaching_value: Visualizes the feedback loop that enables iterative self-improvement in agents, making the abstract concept of 'dynamic memory and self-reflection' concrete by showing how each component feeds into the next.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the concept of self-reflection; use the diagram to walk through each stage of the Reflexion loop step by step.
suggested_caption: Reflexion framework: actor, evaluator, and self-reflection cycle. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across successive trials.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for Reflexion's effectiveness and distinguishes between two distinct failure modes (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), helping students understand what self-reflection specifically addresses.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to ground the theory in experimental results; use to discuss the relative prevalence of hallucination vs. planning failures.
suggested_caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates AlfWorld failures. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, Best) is fed as input, and the model learns to produce progressively improved outputs conditioned on that sequence.
teaching_value: Makes the CoH training procedure tangible by showing how ranked feedback sequences are structured as input, clarifying how the model learns to self-improve by conditioning on its own annotated history.
when_to_show: Introduce when teaching CoH as a self-reflection mechanism; use to contrast with RLHF and highlight how explicit feedback sequences differ from scalar reward signals.
suggested_caption: CoH fine-tuning: model learns from ranked, annotated output sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)

description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): source RL policies generate multi-episode learning histories per task, which are concatenated and used to train a single transformer via behavioral cloning to perform in-context RL.
teaching_value: Clarifies the non-obvious idea that a learning algorithm itself can be distilled into a neural network by showing how multi-episode histories encode the improvement signal that the transformer learns to replicate.
when_to_show: Use when introducing in-context RL and AD; the diagram helps students grasp why multi-episode context is necessary and how the distillation process works.
suggested_caption: Algorithm Distillation distills RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: Performance comparison charts of AD, Expert Distillation (ED), source policy, and RL² on memory-and-exploration environments (dark rooms, watermaze), showing AD matches or exceeds RL² with binary rewards only.
teaching_value: Provides quantitative validation that in-context RL via AD is competitive with dedicated RL algorithms, helping students evaluate the practical viability of the approach across different environment types.
when_to_show: Show after explaining AD to substantiate its claims; prompt students to compare AD vs. RL² and discuss what the results imply about context length requirements.
suggested_caption: AD matches RL² on memory/exploration tasks with binary reward only. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into Sensory Memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), Short-Term/Working Memory, and Long-Term Memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory equivalents.
teaching_value: Bridges cognitive science and AI by mapping biological memory types to their LLM agent counterparts (e.g., in-context window as working memory, vector stores as long-term memory), giving students a framework to reason about agent memory design.
when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the memory module; use the biological analogy to motivate design choices for agent memory systems before discussing technical implementations.
suggested_caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)

description: A recall@10 performance comparison chart of multiple Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) / MIPS algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) plotted against query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff.
teaching_value: Illustrates the practical tradeoff between retrieval accuracy and speed in vector databases, helping students understand why approximate methods are chosen over exact search for large-scale agent memory retrieval.
when_to_show: Show when discussing external memory retrieval mechanisms; use to motivate the choice of ANN over exact search and to compare specific algorithm tradeoffs.
suggested_caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoff. (Google Blog, 2020)

description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how an LLM can orchestrate multiple specialized models as tools, making abstract agent architecture tangible by showing task decomposition, dependency tracking, and multi-model coordination in a single visual flow.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response, reinforcing the four-stage pipeline described in the text.
suggested_caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, and coordinates AI models. (Shen et al. 2023)

description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, call execution, and result evaluation across three levels of tool-use difficulty.
teaching_value: Breaks down the abstract concept of 'tool use' into discrete, evaluable decision points (which API to call, how to parameterize it, whether the result is correct), helping students understand how agent tool-use capability can be benchmarked systematically.
when_to_show: Show after introducing HuggingGPT to deepen understanding of how tool-use decisions are structured and evaluated; use it when discussing how we measure agent accuracy at each step of an API interaction.
suggested_caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision logic for tool selection and execution. (Li et al. 2023)

description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations through a memory stream, retrieves relevant memories via reflection, and uses a planning module to produce actions in a simulated social environment with 25 virtual characters.
teaching_value: Illustrates how memory, reflection, and planning modules combine to produce believable long-term agent behavior, giving students a concrete architectural model for understanding how agents maintain context and simulate human-like cognition over time.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from tool-use to agent simulation topics, or when teaching memory and planning as agent components; use it to ground the abstract idea of 'believable human behavior' in a concrete architectural design.
suggested_caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, reflection, and planning enable human-like behavior. (Park et al. 2023)

description: A side-by-side process diagram contrasting prompt engineering (a mostly one-time, discrete prompt-writing step before inference) with context engineering (an iterative loop that repeatedly curates what information—prompts plus other tokens like retrieved docs, tool outputs, memory—gets passed into the model each run).
teaching_value: Helps learners distinguish scope and workflow: prompts are only one component, while context engineering is an ongoing selection/maintenance problem under a token budget, clarifying why agent reliability depends on repeated curation rather than a single ‘perfect prompt’.
when_to_show: Show when a student assumes better results come only from rewriting the prompt; use it to reframe debugging toward what information is included/excluded each agent step (retrieval results, tool traces, memory, instructions).
suggested_caption: Prompt vs. context engineering: discrete prompt-writing vs iterative context curation (Anthropic).

alt: Calibrating the system prompt in the process of context engineering.
description: A spectrum/continuum diagram for system-prompt calibration: one extreme shows brittle, hardcoded if/else-style prompts; the other shows overly general prompts that assume shared context; the middle implies a calibrated system prompt that is specific, grounded, and robust without overfitting.
teaching_value: Teaches how to tune system prompts for agents by avoiding two common failure modes—overly rigid rules that break on edge cases and vague guidance that leaves the model under-specified—supporting the broader goal of maximizing signal per token in a finite attention budget.
when_to_show: Show when a student’s agent either fails outside narrow cases (too rigid) or behaves inconsistently (too vague); use it to guide rewriting the system prompt toward explicit constraints, definitions, and boundaries without encoding exhaustive if/else logic.
suggested_caption: Calibrating system prompts: avoid brittle rules and overly general assumptions (Anthropic).

description: A flow diagram labeled "Agent Workflow" showing an LLM-driven agent loop: user input goes to an agent/LLM, the agent selects and calls tools, observes tool results, updates state/memory, and iterates until producing a final answer.
teaching_value: Helps learners visualize the iterative control loop behind tool-using agents—planning, acting (tool calls), observing results, and deciding whether to continue—so they can map code (agent executor/graph nodes) to runtime behavior.
when_to_show: Show when a student asks how an agent differs from a single LLM call, or when introducing tool calling as an iterative plan-act-observe loop.
suggested_caption: Agent workflow loop for tool use (LangChain).

description: A diagram titled "LLM augmentations" illustrating an LLM connected to external capabilities such as tools/functions, retrieval over a knowledge base (RAG), memory/state, and possibly structured outputs—showing how inputs/contexts are augmented before generating responses.
teaching_value: Clarifies the main augmentation patterns (tools, retrieval, memory) and how they extend an LLM beyond its base prompt, helping students choose the right augmentation for a task (e.g., factual lookup vs. taking actions).
when_to_show: Show when deciding between RAG, tools, or memory for a requirement (fresh data, actions, personalization).
suggested_caption: Common ways to augment an LLM: tools, retrieval, memory (LangChain).

description: A "Prompt chaining" diagram showing a linear pipeline where the output of one LLM prompt feeds into the next step, forming a multi-step sequence from input to final output.
teaching_value: Demonstrates decomposition of a complex task into sequential sub-tasks, making it easier to explain intermediate representations, debugging points, and why chaining can improve reliability over one-shot prompting.
when_to_show: Show when a student struggles with one-shot prompts and needs a step-by-step pipeline (e.g., extract then transform then summarize).
suggested_caption: Prompt chaining: sequential LLM steps with intermediate outputs (LangChain).

description: A "Parallelization" diagram showing one input branching into multiple LLM/tool tasks executed concurrently, then merging results into a combined final response.
teaching_value: Helps students understand when to run independent subtasks in parallel to reduce latency and improve coverage, and how a fan-out/fan-in pattern aggregates partial results.
when_to_show: Show when optimizing response time or when tasks are independent (e.g., gather facts from multiple sources simultaneously).
suggested_caption: Parallelization: fan out tasks, then aggregate results (LangChain).

description: A "Routing" diagram where an initial classifier/decision step sends the request to one of several specialized branches (different prompts, tools, or agents), each producing an output that becomes the final answer.
teaching_value: Illustrates conditional control flow—how to select the right expert path based on the query—supporting lessons on intent classification, tool selection, and modular system design.
when_to_show: Show when building multi-skill assistants and explaining how to choose between specialized handlers (e.g., math vs. search vs. database).
suggested_caption: Routing: choose a specialized path based on the request (LangChain).

description: An "Orchestrator-worker" diagram showing a central orchestrator that decomposes a task and dispatches subtasks to multiple worker components/agents, then collects their outputs to produce a final result.
teaching_value: Teaches hierarchical coordination: how an orchestrator plans and delegates, why workers can be specialized, and how aggregation enables scalable multi-step solutions.
when_to_show: Show when a student needs to scale beyond a single agent—e.g., splitting research, coding, and verification into separate workers.
suggested_caption: Orchestrator delegates subtasks to workers and aggregates outputs (LangChain).

description: An "Evaluator-optimizer" diagram showing an iterative loop where an optimizer (generator) produces an output, an evaluator critiques/scores it, and feedback is used to refine the next attempt until acceptable quality is reached.
teaching_value: Makes the improvement loop concrete—students can see how critique/metrics drive revisions—supporting instruction on self-reflection, automated evaluation, and iterative refinement strategies.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to improve outputs with critique steps (e.g., rewrite until it meets rubric/constraints).
suggested_caption: Evaluator-optimizer loop for iterative quality improvement (LangChain).

description: An "Agents" diagram depicting an agent centered on an LLM connected to a set of tools and a state/memory component, showing the agent deciding which tool to call and using results to answer the user.
teaching_value: Provides a high-level mental model of an agent as an LLM plus tools plus state, helping learners distinguish agents from simple chains and understand where tool calling fits in the architecture.
when_to_show: Show at the start of an agents lesson or when clarifying the components required to build a tool-using assistant.
suggested_caption: Agent architecture: LLM with tools and state/memory (LangChain).

description: A high-level agent workflow diagram showing a user request going to an LLM-based agent, the agent deciding to call tools, executing tool calls, observing tool results, and iterating until producing a final response.
teaching_value: Helps learners understand the control loop of tool-using agents: plan/decide, act via tools, observe results, and refine before answering.
when_to_show: Show when introducing what an agent is, or when a student is confused about how tool results feed back into the model before the final answer.
suggested_caption: Agent workflow: decide, call tools, observe, iterate (LangChain).

description: A diagram of an LLM augmented by external capabilities, typically including tools/functions, retrieval or knowledge sources, and memory/context, connected around the core model to improve responses.
teaching_value: Clarifies that tool calling is one of several augmentation patterns (alongside retrieval and memory) and shows how external systems extend an LLM beyond its base parameters.
when_to_show: Show when comparing approaches (pure prompting vs tools vs RAG) or when deciding which augmentation to use for a task.
suggested_caption: Common LLM augmentations: tools, retrieval, and memory (LangChain).

description: A prompt-chaining diagram showing multiple sequential LLM steps where the output of one prompt becomes the input to the next, forming a linear pipeline to reach a final result.
teaching_value: Demonstrates decomposition: breaking a complex task into smaller, verifiable LLM steps, which improves reliability and makes intermediate reasoning artifacts inspectable.
when_to_show: Show when a student’s single prompt is failing and you want to teach splitting the task into sequential stages with checkpoints.
suggested_caption: Prompt chaining: sequential LLM steps with intermediate outputs (LangChain).

description: A parallelization diagram where a task is split into multiple branches processed concurrently (often by multiple LLM calls or workers) and then aggregated into a combined result.
teaching_value: Teaches how to reduce latency and improve coverage by running independent subtasks in parallel and then merging, rather than relying on one monolithic model call.
when_to_show: Show when optimizing for speed or breadth (e.g., generating multiple candidates, evaluating options, or researching multiple facets simultaneously).
suggested_caption: Parallelization: fan-out work, then aggregate results (LangChain).

description: A routing diagram showing an input being classified or inspected and then directed to one of several specialized paths/models/tools, with the selected route producing the output.
teaching_value: Helps learners see how to build conditional logic into LLM systems—choosing the right tool/model/prompt based on the request type to improve accuracy and cost.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to handle heterogeneous queries (e.g., math vs policy vs retrieval) by selecting specialized handlers.
suggested_caption: Routing: send requests to specialized paths based on intent (LangChain).

description: A worker pattern diagram showing a coordinator distributing tasks to one or more workers (often LLM calls or tool-executing nodes) and collecting their outputs.
teaching_value: Illustrates scalable orchestration: separating coordination from execution so multiple workers can handle subtasks, retries, or tool interactions robustly.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how to scale an agent workflow to many subtasks or how to structure a graph with coordinator and worker nodes.
suggested_caption: Worker pattern: coordinator assigns tasks to workers and collects outputs (LangChain).

description: An evaluator-optimizer loop diagram where an output is evaluated (by rules or an LLM judge), feedback is produced, and an optimizer revises the solution iteratively until it meets criteria.
teaching_value: Teaches iterative improvement and self-correction: using evaluation signals to refine outputs, which is key for quality control in tool-using or multi-step systems.
when_to_show: Show when a student needs higher reliability (e.g., formatting constraints, correctness checks) and you want to introduce evaluation + revision cycles.
suggested_caption: Evaluator-optimizer: evaluate outputs, then revise iteratively (LangChain).

description: An agent diagram showing an LLM agent connected to a set of tools, with a loop indicating repeated tool selection and execution before returning a final answer to the user.
teaching_value: Reinforces the core idea of tool calling: the model can choose actions (tools) dynamically, observe results, and continue until it can answer confidently.
when_to_show: Show when first defining an agent versus a simple chain, or when explaining how tool availability changes what the model can do.
suggested_caption: Agent with tools: select actions, observe results, answer (LangChain).
